item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got chills several times while reading this. A sign of a good horror skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhh apparently I hadn't upvoted this yet! This is a personal favorite of mine and I love the story it tells.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This shit right here is like, a horror that is so close and personal to me, it really works..

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the point it said "it appears to be most common among adults raised as an only child," I went "ohhh, shit. I see where this is going." I was not wrong. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this one really gets under my skin. in a good way. the fact that we don't know what happened to Cragglewood Park or the trees within makes me even more upset. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never been on a merry-go-round in my life and this made me grateful I never did. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my all time favorite SCPs right here, if not the favorite, purely because it is one of the few SCps that legitimately gave me an existential crisis the first time I read it. The buildup is flawless and pays off well in the end. You can tell it is good by how much everyone keeps asking to know more about it.

Plus, as a twin who is very close to their sibling and grew up with memory problems when they were younger this is basically my nightmare scenario.

Incredible work. Upvoted.

item.title: Solid +1... with some questions.
item.content:encoded:
Wow.

Wow. I was not expecting this when I saw that first image.

This scip was phenomenal in more ways than 1. Definitely an upvote.

However, I have but three questions.

1. How do the victims know what they have to do?

2. What happens if the victims refuse and try to escape?

3. In your previous tree scip, you mention that campers have gone missing. If that's the case, then this really isn't just a location, isn't it? The carousel might not be a carousel either.

Either way, great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) That's up for the reader to figure out; I'll at least say that when the Foundation claims something doesn't exist, they typically have pretty good reasons to do so.

2) Yes.

3) Whatever this place is, it's not chained to geography (note this anomaly happens all around the world). Parents aren't there because the park doesn't bring them there.

4) The last lines of the final video log implies what happens to those children who get on the merry-go-round. ETA: In case that's too ambiguous, I'll clarify: No, they don't get to leave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Does that (and the video cassette) mean that SCP-2571 is an actual park and not just memories?

Subjects attended the park with numerous other children, none of whom they knew.

Are some of these children "taken siblings"?

No adult supervision was present.

Does that mean that The Craddlewood park is an actual place somewhere on Earth, but it can't be perceived or entered by adults, and children just accidentally entered there and their parents/supervisors forgot them?

The park featured only one ride; a carousel (or 'merry-go-round'). Although children accompanying the subjects boarded this ride, the subjects themselves did not.

Does that mean that those who boarded this merry-go-round can never leave The Craddlewood Park?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Antimemetic, in this context, specifically refers to the twist:

SPOILERS
– hide block

That each victim isn't an "only child"; they actually had siblings that the park took and they're forgetting/ignoring existed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
antimemetic

Does that mean that this is not just anomalous memories, but the actual anomalous park with antimemetic camouflage?
One of my favorite skips now has a very well deserved 300 upvotes!

item.title: Woah this is creepy
item.content:encoded: Terrifying,This SCP made me jump when I realised the true sick story the SCPF has yet to uncover.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was reading the description and getting this super-happy skip-what-does-good vibe from it. Like it would turn out that it was a park that would make friends with lonely kids and boost their self-esteem or something. Didn't like that vibe at all. Then the first log opened up with "just this nightmare I used to have". Good stuff.

"have you always been an only child?" is a good wham line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't read the explanations here, but when reading the last line of the last interview made me scroll up and down through the previous ones, deducing suddenly that these must be children whose older siblings had been aborted, about which they were never told.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, looks like I'm not going to bed tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's because I spoiled the images myself and decided to avoid them in my reading by viewing the video log in the page source, but this skip is more like a tear jerker to me than nightmare fuel, especially with that video log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed, and I think that's Hippo's greatest strength as a storyteller (along with just plain skillful writing) - for lack of a better word, subtlety. It's something I've always strived for in my writing - knowing where it's coming from for me, and trying to pull emotions from the reader's own experiences, while not pushing my own interpretation of my writing on them. I think it's an incredibly delicate balance to find, and I see a lot of otherwise fantastic well regarded articles on this site that still push that tipping point too far (and result in comments like "I would have rather not been beaten over the head with the message").

I'm just rambling too (and probably off topic - sorry, I'm new to commenting here) but I also greatly appreciated Hippo's comment, both as a storytelling insight and personal catharsis on my part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got to comment on this revelation from the author.

One of the great strengths of this article is that you still get the horror without seeing how all the parts fit together. I did not see the child abuse angle when I first read it, but that was because I didn't have any experience with it between myself and my siblings for me to recognize the signs. But on a reread, yes, I saw it.

I think it's important to recognize that some articles do exactly that; they pull on experiences the author had, experienced, or were exposed to, and those deeper meanings might not be readily apparent to other readers who haven't experienced similar. It can cause an article to have different feels for different readers. So, I always ask myself: does this article convey what it's trying to project on me? Or is it not conveying the same message because I'm not the target the article is speaking to?

I'm really just rambling more than anything, but part of good storycraft is understanding how different readers with different experiences might interpret the same situation. Sometimes, you want to try to ensure everyone gets the same reading from it. Sometimes, you want to let those different interpretations dominate.

This is good storycraft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was really cagey about saying this initially because I didn't want to shape how people read it (or create an environment where people would feel bad for downvoting it), but seeing how it's been almost a year now, I feel comfortable stating it: This is intended as an allegory for child abuse (of all types), and how one child will sometimes take that abuse for the sake of another.

On one hand, I'm very glad to hear that it actually managed to connect to you on that level! On the other hand, I mean — I'm not glad that you've had experiences like that in the first place. :/

Regardless, I really appreciate the comment — thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been lurking for 7 years, recently finally made an account, and this is my first comment ever to just say that out of the hundreds (thousands?) of SCPs I've read, this is the most disturbing to me, on a profound level. I'm sure it's mostly personal, due to severe partially repressed childhood trauma, and my role as the "protector" eldest sibling (can you tell I've been in a lot of therapy?). But my issues aren't the point, it's the fact that you tapped into it on such a pure, primal level. It actually brought back a recurring childhood nightmare of mine which I was never able to put a finger on why it was so disturbing. I thought of this article for days after I read it.

Anyway, I mean all of that to be read as high praise. I love all of your other SCPs, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second to last grinning tree is from there, yep! And that tree you linked was actually one of the ones I considered using, but couldn't find a good place to fit it in.

ETA: Also that mirror bit was pretty fantastic. Like an engine for childhood trauma. xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm from the Netherlands and I believe that the grinning tree in the second-to-last image in the article might be from there, though I don't think the open-mouthed smiling one from the final image is, and thank goodness for that.

It also has this tree which people often find creepy, which isn't helped by it being a very complex and detailed animatronic.

Oh, and also my personal childhood trauma (English subs available).

Either way, Efteling definitely has a bunch of good SCP fodder if you can find an appropriately licensed picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I first read this SCP, I didn't make the connections that it stole siblings, or siblings sacrificed themselves to protect their siblings, then they became trees, and erased all memories of those siblings until I read the discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! Also:

(seriously with that final image: the drawing used as the main page image is tagged as 'creepy' and 'scary' in the source but that hell-demon of a final image which is almost even [b]scarier[/b] without the distortion is not?)

Yeah, it's from Efteling, I think — which is an amusement park in the Netherlands just full of creepy things like that. I suspect it's not labeled as 'creepy' because in the Netherlands this is just how all their trees look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those articles that is much better on the second read than on the first because you catch all of those things you didn't the first time, such as the "why did he hug me I didn't even know him" bit in the second log.

On the first read I thought you might have overdone it with the people being scared and traumatized in the interviews but the gut punch that is the realization of what the park is actually doing combined with the terrifying image of that tree in the final image made it work. It's a short and to-the-point parade of terror without losing out on the buildup.

(seriously with that final image: the drawing used as the main page image is tagged as 'creepy' and 'scary' in the source but that hell-demon of a final image which is almost even scarier without the distortion is not?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sympathize. This SCP is the perfect amount of NOPE. That is to say a WHOLE LOT OF IT.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
aaaa D:

I MEAN uh I'm glad that the story worked for you but I hope it did not cause you too much stress and fear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the most scared I've been of an SCP since I first discovered the site 5 or so years ago. When I first read this, a few months ago, I was literally tearing up from sheer stress and fear.

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: Oops; thanks — fixed!

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: These are not the droids you're looking for. Move along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is so fucking scary, mainly because of the implications behind it. I feel terrible imagining what would happen if this were real. Forgetting my brother exists is just terrifying to me. +1, nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's how nightmares work a lot of the time, especially recurring nightmares. You remember how it goes, but you're not lucid dreaming so you can't control it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't have a clue what was going on at first.
Then I came to the discussion page.
Then I pretty much had nightmares (thankfully not of Cragglewood Park)
Once I figured out what was going on, this was terrifying. One of the few scips on the sight that genuinely triggers adult fear rather than only managing to pull off more irrational fears or jumpscares.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this in the draft stage, love it here. This is terrifying, but not in a "fridge horror" way like SCP-2000 or SCP-3003. This is upfront, bone-chillingly spooky like SCP-087.

+1

item.title: Fucking Trees
item.content:encoded:
I knew those bastards were up to something.

They just sit there, "photosynthesising". Acting like they're not doing anything bad. So suspicious aren't they? They're not only putting chemicals in the water, they're eating the fucking kids too!

shakes fist CURSE YOU TREES! I KNEW YOU WERE EVIL!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There goes a half hour of my life I'll never get back…lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last link was meant to be from the proper timed position. Ugh…sorry. For those of you still interested in the image's origin, here's a You-Tube search for the old commercials.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderfully terse. You get a lot of mileage out of really small and short logs. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first thing I jumped to reading this one and -437 was "What the hell do dryads want all those kids for…?" At least I'm not the only one thinking it sounds like something fae. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So based on some of the other theories posted here, this is effectively a memory replacing meme that spreads through children who lost a sibling at some point, rather than a physical event. But is still able to physically manifest an old VHS tape. Or maybe it's overwriting an existing one? I'm still trying to figure this part out. Perhaps it's loosely connected to SCP-1981?

The crosslink to SCP-437 confuses me a little, maybe it's the same phenomena behind them, some sort of tree themed entities stealing children and putting them inside trees, or wearing(?) their faces for their weird nightmare carnival.

Or faeries, this seems like something they would do.

Also: Jesus Christ that last image, it looks like something from my nightmares. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 just for the Fun Fruits image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once again, I missed a good one.

What the shit, dude? Wow.

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: SunnyClockwork recently drew this excellent art of SCP-2571!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this confused me.

Did the kids GO, physically, in a moment of their infancies? if so, 3 million people is far too much. A large city where all kids went with this one sibling to this same park.

If they metaphysically went, then I totally agree.

By the way I see a lot of discussion about buildup… maybe they were from before the edits. Your skippos are unbelievably well paced. I just said meh until i oponed the logs and went increasedly freaked out, like in the old classical creepy pastas. I hope to write like you one day

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice work. I don't feel like I need to add any more people into the pool of "childhoods riddled with oddly cartoonish yet terrifying, infuriating nightmares" because it did of course amp up how much I enjoyed this and it did of course made me crap myself.

+ show block
– hide block

What I have to say, is that I find the Procedure very interesting, and it got me thinking a hell lot, I even amounted a kinda dumb headcanon. I believe the Procedure might also have to do with the growth in numbers of these cases, and other things detailed in the Description. We all know this new trend in forums and general internet interaction, the famous Mandela Effect; now if you've seen posts or people's experience with this effect, you know the general outcome, people relate, feel weird and investigate until someone finds the reason for that out of place memory, or don't.

So MTF is professionally tasked with actually monitoring text communities and those kinds of posts (using the appropiately named MANDELA bot) for case details and such, but I think they might be missing the point that perhaps all these people discussing their horrifying Mandela dreams on the Internet is actually triggering every damn only child on the planet, seeing as how the Foundation has amounted the HUGE number of 0.5%, and still growing. I guess that's about 70% - 30% people who got triggered on Reddit and others who just remember, respectively. However, I think it gets even more specific than that, I do not think in the least that Cragglewood is a fucked up Joker-style child abduction place, or connected to SPC-437, I think it's a position your mind takes when you as a child between 4 and 12 loose an, also young, close sibling. If Cragglewood took 0.5% of the world's population's big bros the world would be full in code red alert for all parents in the world, but parents don't seem to be out of key at all, they don't say anything about this place or about their older child suddenly dissapearing. You know, it might even sound more fucked up than children becoming singing trees forever, but perhaps it's what an anxious childhood mind gets in his head when your parents come to your room and give you the bad news about your loved sibling going to a better place.

So what is Randolph's tape? I think that's SCP-2571, just like that one (I agree, very awesome) Reagan SPC tape the author mentioned. What I believe is that the content's of that tape are implanted into minds of children everywhere (after the event of finding out the death of a sibling) as a nightmare/memory, with all the statics and shots of plain gravel left to the kid's own fears and imagination, a whole gravel path with variable types of music, trees, kids, concluded with one sad goodbye on a carousel, and a very scary reality at the end, that your brother is gone no matter what, oddly represented by a forever singing tree.

So you either go crazy about the nightmare for ever, (like Randolph) or you forget all about the brother you loved thereafter, and just think it's another dumb dream… Until you find an absurdly dangerous Reddit post which takes you back to that terrible place, but it's probably just another weird Mandela Effect, isn't it? So you just post your own memory of it, not knowing you're kind of posting your own version of how your mind took your sibling's death.


Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Huh, now it's creepy and depressing, sorry if I bored anyone with my out of place theory, but I'm trying to showcase the author that articles like these, where they get you to think outside it's box and get your own belief about it, are the best. You really nailed that part where you give just enough "not enough information" for us to come up with things, as a new member that decided to get in just from thinking about other articles, it's the best element you can have in yours. and for that, I +1 this and support your next projects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Deeply unsettling and reminiscent of how one reacts to bad childhood memories. This doesn't just remind me of how I felt when I had nightmares as a kid — it reminds me a lot of how I felt after like a fight with my little sister when I knew I was in the wrong. A kind of dread and guilt that it's too late to fix it? Not sure, but I find this unsettling on a lot of levels.

This was very well-written, exemplary work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This did very little for me I'm afraid, so I suspect there is some fundamental experience I'm missing that's causing everyone to like it. I never had nightmares or anything as a kid, so maybe it's that.
It's well written and well paced (even if I did find the interview logs a touch melodramatic), but I don't get the immediate fear amongst the children. How did they get to this place? Why are they all immediately terrified? How do they apparently know what's going to happen? If they do know, why are they all meekly following the path instead of running?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…. Now that I think I understand what's going on here, I think I can say that this is a definite upvote. For some reason the whole "fake childhood memory" thing has been used a lot recently, but the way this scip affects these people is what makes it stand out, imo (also that last image in the video log, holy fucking shit that is terrifying).

+1

item.title: Insert your own witty Circus reference or tune here
item.content:encoded:
SUBJECT: One of the kids. One of the kids that got on.
REINER: Yes?
SUBJECT: Why? Why did he hug me? I didn't — I don't even know who he was —

The reveal on the relationship with the kids only twisted the knife in deeper. The article ain’t perfect (the video log felt a bit too by the numbers, if you don’t my saying so) and a bit much is left unsaid, things that feel likethe Foundation should know in some capacity (like, the location of this carousel, or anything related to how the trees grab kids), but I’m not going to complain about what an article doesn’t or didn’t have in favor of what it does, and what it has is pretty good.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple things that basically boil down to 'I like it a lot'.

1. It makes me smile seeing an author respond as constructively and maturely to criticism as you have in these discussions, Hippo. Don't want to make a huge deal of it, but it goes against most peoples' first instincts (including mine) to react that way to criticism and I thought it deserved acknowledging.

2. I really like the crosslink. There are some inconsistent details between the two, but in a good way - clearly this scp does not in any way explain the earlier one. The reader can accept both articles as part of their headcannon without being forced to believe anything in particular about the SCPs involved.

Everything else I liked has been said better by others. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Interesting. I like the idea of this. I think it borrows a bit to much from other SCPs, but the execution really sells it. The addition of the images in the video segment really help the unsettling factor. Also nice Candle Cove reference.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You definitely don't have an obligation to explain why you don't like it (although I also definitely appreciate you taking the time to do so!). A lot of the stuff you're bringing up has answers in my head, but I always feel super-strange about defending my narrative choices when someone doesn't like them; if you don't like them, you don't like them, and defending them seems a bit of a silly exercise on my part.

That being said, every choice you're describing was deliberate; like a lot of my horror skips, this story is an allegory for a banal horror that terrifies me. I picked a carousel for a reason; I decided to have a locked room everyone refuses to acknowledge for a reason — etc. Nothing I went with here was selected just because it's spoopy (except maybe the Craggles being trees; I really just went with that because I found pictures that worked).

I also put just as much effort into this skip as I put into any of my skips! I think what you see as a lack of time is probably just this story falling flat on its face for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right. However, the use of antimemes is often not much more than a convenient plot device. That's not necessarily a bad thing in and of itself – a certain number of convenient plot devices are always required to arrive at the setting of the SCP universe, where paranormal things are happening constantly but the public don't notice. (Or any fictional universe, really.) If the implied antimemetic component were the only example of a too-convenient, slightly implausible plot device, I wouldn't bat an eyelid. My objection was that there were too many such 'deus ex machina' elements here – I wasn't objecting to the fact that there were any at all. It was the weight of these in aggregate which broke past my (admittedly low) plausibility threshold, straining my suspension of disbelief and making the composition feel lazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
millions of people just ignoring a child's bedroom full of stuff in their own houses

Isn't that why it's antimemetic? It is tagged antimemetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like really clichéd, painting-by-numbers work to me. Theme park, disappearing kids, dream-that-was-actually-real – I've seen these elements so many times in scips. This whole piece feels like it was written quite thoughtlessly.

Let me elaborate. I mean, I don't want to twist the knife here, but this has a very successful rating, so I feel that a) it can withstand the upcoming criticism, and b) I have a greater obligation to explain why I don't like it. There were a few deus ex machina plot devices: the convenient tape (how did some seven-year-old smuggle in, and then operate, a camcorder during this event?), millions of people just ignoring a child's bedroom full of stuff in their own houses, the kids knowing what the trees wanted. Also, the elements didn't feel at all cohesive. The carousel, evil trees, and disappearances was just grab-bag spoopy for spoopy's sake. Structure was suboptimal - for instance, the disappearing kids twist was obvious to me from a mile off, and in another example of formulaic writing, the log at the end doesn't feel like it adds anything, other than being a perfunctory step of the "how to get as many upvotes as possible in a scip" recipe. Finally, and fatally, the showcase scary imagery just did nothing for me. It's… just a carousel, man. Just a plain-ass, damn carousel. The horses aren't even crying, or covered in bees, or anything.

It's weird, Hippo - with your stuff, either I love it, or I find it dumb and trite as hell. This one goes in the second box, unfortunately. I think I enjoy your work when you've spent more time on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*INTERNAL SCREAMING*
HERES THE +1
TAKE IT TAKE IIIIII…..
*starts to sing*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Geez there sure is something about people getting turned into trees that creeps me the fuck out, take your +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m guessing this implys that it isn’t even a theme park, and the only reason it looks so cutesy is that the children see it as that?
